title: Government uses AI to help it buy, but humans still get the final say
full_text:
For just $1000 in AI tokens, the Digital Transformation Agency achieved what previously took 18 months and up to 40 staff: evaluating more than 3000 companies vying for lucrative government technology contracts.
The test, which took just 7½ hours, means the agency can now open the government-wide panel for IT consulting and labour hire firms more frequently, chief executive Chris Fechner says.
However, human evaluators are still required to double-check the tool, which disagreed with public servants almost one-third of the time. A second public servant is called in to act as a tie-breaker when there is disagreement between the initial human and AI.
The automation push comes as the government faces a massive tech delivery backlog, with one-third of its $10 billion pipeline of digital projects reporting a “medium or lower delivery confidence” in the next six months. A projected shortage of about 8000 digital workers in the public service by 2030 is adding to the problem.
Fechner told The Australian Financial Review Digital Economy Forum in Sydney that government procurement haa historically been hampered by heavy due diligence requirements.
“[The DTA] manages the vast majority of the digital purchasing that goes on across government ... and one of the things that we struggle with is how frequently we can open [the Digital Marketplace] panel,” he says.
“The last time we opened the panel, it actually took about 18 months [to evaluate], and we want to have that panel open multiple times a year to get new vendors on that panel.”
The agency, working with the CSIRO, trained its AI model using thousands of successful and hundreds of unsuccessful applications from the earlier panel.
“What we actually found was only about 30 per cent of all of those [applications] we evaluated had a difference between what the AI came out with and what the two humans that previously evaluated was,” Fechner says.
Despite the 30 per cent discrepancy between the AI and human evaluators, the agency is pressing ahead with its use in the current digital marketplace panel. He says the new AI-driven evaluations can be up to 60 per cent faster.
“[It] was also really important that we had a human [who] was making those decisions still, and that we had control over it, and there was actually a human response if people wanted to understand why or why it didn’t work,” he says.
The agency is now helping smaller vendors, which make up about 70 per cent of the panel, overcome the onerous compliance burden of joining a federal buying panel.
“[In] our first version of the Digital Marketplace, about a quarter of the applicants fell out at the first hurdle because they didn’t provide their statement of tax compliance,” Fechner says. “We found that that was a completely unacceptable outcome.”
The system now allows vendors to provide information at a later date without being knocked out of the application process, to ensure the agency can “actually see what their capabilities are, not how well they can tick the boxes”.
$10 billion pipeline of projects
Regarding the $10 billion pipeline of work, Fechner says the broad lessons are “government needs to be a better systems integrator, a better composer of solutions, and we need to be able to chunk our solutions up more”.
That means project management skills are as critical as knowing how to program.
“Historically, what has happened is projects have been approved so quickly that the only option is to go to market to get those resources, and the market will always have a place in delivering those resources,” he says.
Instead of hiring thousands of developers, agencies would be better off with more public servants who “can manage outcomes, manage projects, manage products [and] improve our data capabilities”.
YXJ0aWNsZTo1NjUyOWQ3Yi01NDk1LTRkYWUtOTFkNy1kM2UwOTBlODVkNGE

author: Edmund Tadros
heading: Data Science and AI
